description: Nuts do not appear until the tree is about 30 years old. The 4-ribbed nut is about an inch long, roughly spherical, with a thin shell. The meat of the nut is very bitter. The husk is four-winged from the tip to about the middle and is covered with yellowish-green, scruffy hairs. Nuts display in October.
origin: Eastern Canada to Central   Eastern United States.
synonyms: ["Hicorius cordiformis var. latifolia", "Hicorius cordiformis var. elongata", "Carya pubescens", "Hicorius minima", "Juglans mucronata", "Juglans alba var. minima", "Scoria minima", "Hicorius cordiformis", "Carya sulcata", "Scoria mucronata"]

care.used_for_details: Said to be somewhat inferior to the other hickories, but is used for the same purposes: tool handles, furniture, interior paneling, and sporting goods. Also used to smoke ham and bacon.
care.edible_parts: Thin shelled nut, but bitter and astringent.

description: Carya glabra, the pignut hickory, is a common, but not abundant species of hickory in the oak-hickory forest association in the Eastern United States and Canada. Other common names are pignut, sweet pignut, coast pignut hickory, smoothbark hickory, swamp hickory, and broom hickory. The pear-shaped nut ripens in September and October, has a sweet maple like smell, and is an important part of the diet of many wild animals.
origin: South Eastern Canada to Central   Southeastern U.S.A

description: The pecan is a species of hickory native to the Southern United States and northern Mexico in the region of the Mississippi River.
origin: Central   E. Central U.S.A. to Mexico

care.watering_guide: Missouri BG: Best grown in humusy, rich, moist, well-drained soils in full sun. Difficult to transplant because of its deep taproot. If grown for nut production, plant at least two different varieties for best cross-pollination. Nut production can be sparse in the northern part of its growing range, particularly

common_pests: Aphids, pecan weevils, stink bugs, twig girdlers, and fall webworms can cause some issues.

description: Carya ovata, the shagbark hickory, is a common hickory native to eastern North America, with two varieties. The trees can grow to quite a large size but are unreliable in their fruit output. The nut is consumed by wildlife and historically by Native Americans, who also used the wood.
origin: E. Canada to Central   E. U.S.A. and NE. Mexico
synonyms: ["Juglans alba var. ovata", "Hicorius ovata", "Juglans alba ovata", "Scoria ovata", "Carya alba subsp. ovata", "Juglans ovata"]

care.soil_types: Slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil
care.fertilizer_type: Apply fertilizers once to twice to young tree every year
care.light_guide: NC State: Deep shade (Less than 2 hours to no direct sunlight) | Climate: temperate
care.tips: Remove diseased and dead leaves timely | Similar plants: Juglans nigra | Good companions: Loblolly Pine, Red Oak, Sugar maple, White Oak, White Pine | Native to: VRG OO, TEX OO, KAN OO, INI, RHO OO
care.used_for_details: Used as fuelwood, charcoal, furniture, flooring, tool handles, ladder rungs, dowels, athletic good and gymnasium equipment. Wood is used to cure meats.

description: Carya pallida, sand hickory, or pale hickory is a species of hickory native to the southeastern United States. It is a perennial, dicotyledonous plant which prefers rocky or sandy habitats. The sand hickory can reach heights of up to 30m, but its typical height is between 9-24m. In an open area, Carya crowns are usually towering and slim. The sand hickory nut is edible and consumed by various organisms.
synonyms: ["Hicorius pallida var. apposita", "Hicorius pallida var. arenicola", "Carya pallida var. apposita", "Hicorius pallida", "Hicorius pallida var. pyriformis", "Hicorius pallida var. arkansana", "Carya pallida var. arenicola", "Carya pallida var. pyriformis", "Hicorius pallida var. villosa"]

care.tips: Similar plants: Acer pensylvanicum | Native to: LOU OO, MSO OO, INI, GEO, TEN

care.used_for_details: The wood is heavy, hard, and tough and has been used for tool handles.
care.edible_parts: The seed can be eaten, raw or cooked, and has a sweet flavor.

description: Carya texana is a North American tree in the walnut family, Juglandaceae. It is endemic to the United States, found primarily in the southern Great Plains and the Lower Mississippi Valley. It is an endangered species in Indiana, where it occurs in the southwest corner of the state.
Engelhardia roxburghiana is a tree in the family Juglandaceae. It is named for the Scottish botanist William Roxburgh.
origin: MLY PM, ASS ME, EHM AP, SUM OO, CHC CQ
synonyms: ["Juglans pterococca", "Engelhardia pterococca", "Engelhardia polystachya", "Alfaroa roxburghiana", "Engelhardia formosana", "Alfaropsis roxburghiana", "Engelhardia chrysolepis", "Engelhardia wallichiana", "Engelhardia spicata var. formosana", "Engelhardia fenzlii"]

description: Juglans californica, the California black walnut, also called the California walnut, or the Southern California black walnut, is a large shrub or small tree of the walnut family, Juglandaceae, endemic to the Central Valley and the Coast Range valleys from Northern to Southern California.
origin: MEXICO, MXN BC, MXN BS, MXN SO, TZK

ja: カリフォルニア・ウォルナット

description: Juglans cinerea, commonly known as butternut or white walnut, is a species of walnut native to the eastern United States and southeast Canada.
origin: Eastern Canada to North Central and Eastern United States

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
common_problems: The tree is endangered in its native range due to butternut canker disease and overharvesting.
The canker has devastated the tree and there is no cure.
It may also suffer from blight.
This tree is also susceptible to fire and storm damage.
common_pests: Potential insects include wood borers, nut weevils, lace bugs, husk flies, bark beetles, and butternut curculio.
The common grackle has been known to destroy immature fruit, and they are classified as pests.
care.used_for: ["The wood is used to make furniture", "cabinetry", "instrument cases", "interior woodwork", "hand-carved wall panels", "trim", "church decoration", "and altars. The nut is popularly used in New England for making maple-butternut candy.  Early settlers used fruit husks and inner bark to make orange or yellow dye an"]
care.used_for_details: The wood is used to make furniture, cabinetry, instrument cases, interior woodwork, hand-carved wall panels, trim, church decoration, and altars. The nut is popularly used in New England for making maple-butternut candy.  Early settlers used fruit husks and inner bark to make orange or yellow dye an
care.edible_parts: Nuts are edible and sought after by food enthusiasts. The nuts are sweet, oily, and mild flavored. They can become rancid quickly.

description: Juglans major, also known as Arizona walnut, is a walnut tree which grows to 50 ft tall (15 m) with a DBH of up to 0.61 metres (2 ft) at elevations of 300–2,130 m (1,000–7,000 ft) in Texas, Oklahoma, New Mexico, Arizona, and Utah. It also occurs in Mexico as far south as Guerrero. Common names include Arizona black walnut, and the Spanish name nogal cimarrón.
origin: TCS, NCS DA, MXE AG, TCS AR, NCS ST
synonyms: ["Juglans rupestris var. major", "Juglans microcarpa var. major", "Juglans microcarpa subsp. major"]

description: Juglans mandshurica, also known as Manchurian walnut or tigernut, is a species of deciduous tree in the walnut family, Juglandaceae. It is native to East Asia.
origin: TCS AR, CHN SA, NCS ST, NORTHEASTERN USA, AMU OO

description: Juglans microcarpa, known also as the little walnut, Texas walnut, Texas black walnut or little black walnut, is a large shrub or small tree which grows wild along streams and ravines in Texas, New Mexico, Oklahoma, and Kansas, and the northernmost states of Mexico. It produces nuts with a width of 1/2—3/4 in. The pinnately compound leaves bear 7—25 untoothed to finely-toothed leaflets, each 1/4—1/2 in wide. It is found at elevations ranging from 700 ft to 6700 ft.
origin: MEXICO, MXE ZA, NORTH CENTRAL USA, NWM OO, KAN

description: Juglans neotropica is a species of plant in the Juglandaceae family. It is found in Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. It is threatened by habitat loss. Common names include Colombian walnut, Ecuadorian walnut, Andean walnut, nogal, cedro negro, cedro nogal, and nogal Bogotano.
origin: VEN, PER, GAL OO, PER OO, CLM OO

description: Juglans nigra, the eastern American black walnut, is a species of deciduous tree in the walnut family, Juglandaceae, native to central and eastern North America, growing mostly in riparian zones.
origin: Eastern United States, west to Texas, and South Eastern Canada
synonyms: ["Wallia nigra", "Juglans pitteursii", "Juglans rugosa", "Juglans nigra oblonga", "Juglans nigra f. oblonga", "Juglans nigra f. laciniata"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
care.tips: Good companions: Allium ampeloprasum, Black elderberry, Black locust, Black mulberry, Black raspberry, Gooseberry, Honey locust, Lycium barbarum, Parsley, Pedunculate Oak | Similar plants: Carya glabra
care.toxicity_note: Toxic to Dogs, Toxic to Horses Non-Toxic to Cats. Signs: Dogs: moldy nuts/hulls can cause tremors and seizures, ingestion of shavings can cause incoordination; Horses: laminitis, colic
care.toxicity_symptoms: Dogs: moldy nuts/hulls can cause tremors and seizures, ingestion of shavings can cause incoordination; Horses: laminitis, colic
care.toxicity_first_aid: Remove any remaining plant material from mouth. Rinse with water. Monitor for symptoms (vomiting, drooling, lethargy). Call poison control or vet if symptoms appear.
care.used_for_details: Yellow dye made from fruit husks, seed is used in candy-making, cleaning abrasives, and explosives. Heartwood is of superior quality and value; heavy, hard, and strong with very little warping as it dries. Highly prized for furniture, cabinets, etc.
care.good_companions: ["Allium ampeloprasum", "Black elderberry", "Black locust", "Black mulberry", "Black raspberry", "Gooseberry", "Honey locust", "Lycium barbarum", "Parsley", "Pedunculate Oak"]

description: Juglans regia, known by various common names including the common walnut, English walnut, or Persian walnut amongst other names, is a species of walnut. It is native to Eurasia in at least southwest and central Asia and southeast Europe, but its exact natural area is obscure due to its long history of cultivation.
synonyms: ["Juglans salicifolia", "Juglans regia var. maxima", "Juglans regia var. oblonga", "Juglans regia var. angulosa", "Juglans regia f. fruticosa", "Juglans regia f. pendula", "Juglans filicifolia", "Juglans duclouxiana", "Juglans regia laciniata", "Juglans regia lusus heterophylla"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
care.toxicity_note: Human: allergenic. Animal: weak toxic. Parts: Whole plant. (Source: TPPT/Agroscope)
common_problems: The English Walnut has no serious insect or disease problems.
Anthracnose, bacterial blight, root rot, canker, leaf spot, and shoot dieback can occur.
The roots of this tree produce a chemical known as juglones.
This chemical is toxic to other plants including azaleas, rhododendron, blueberries, peonies, tomatoes, peppers, and potatoes.
The litter from the nuts can be messy in the fall.
common_pests: The English Walnut has no serious insect or disease problems.
Potential insects include webworms and caterpillars that like to chew on the foliage.
care.used_for: ["The English Walnut is grown commercially for its edible nuts.  The wood is used to build furniture", "cabinets", "and gunstocks. The English Walnut was a folk remedy for a multitude of ailments including anthrax", "asthma", "backache", "colic", "and rheumatism."]
care.used_for_details: The English Walnut is grown commercially for its edible nuts.  The wood is used to build furniture, cabinets, and gunstocks. The English Walnut was a folk remedy for a multitude of ailments including anthrax, asthma, backache, colic, and rheumatism.
care.edible_parts: The nuts are edible. They may be eaten fresh, roasted, and salted.

description: Platycarya strobilacea is a species of flowering plant in the family Juglandaceae, formerly treated as comprising the single living species in Platycarya, though a second living species Platycarya longzhouensis is now recognized. It is native to eastern Asia in China, Vietnam, Korea, and Japan.

Pterocarya fraxinifolia is a species of tree in the Juglandaceae family. It is commonly known as the Caucasian wingnut or Caucasian walnut. It is native to the Caucasian region Armenia, Azerbaijan, Georgia, Iran, Russia, Ukraine and Turkey. It was introduced to France in 1784, and to Great Britain after 1800.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Pterocarya rhoifolia, commonly known as Japanese wingnut, is a species of tree in the family Juglandaceae. It is widely distributed throughout Japan, and also found in China in the Laoshan District of eastern Shandong Province. Its natural presence in China is contested.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Pterocarya stenoptera, the Chinese wingnut, is a small-winged wingnut tree of the Juglandaceae family. It is originally from Southeast China.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
